13: A type: 'Abraham lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, behind him was a ram, caught in a thicket by his horns. And Abraham went and took the ram and offered it up as a burnt offering instead of his son.' The substitutionary ram is the most explicit type of substitutionary atonement in the OT. The ram dies in Isaac's place: the innocent substituted for the guilty who was also the beloved son. The location (Moriah, later Jerusalem) and the typological pattern (father giving son, son willingly going, ram substituted) form the OT's clearest foreshadowing of the cross. The Aqedah is the type; Calvary is the antitype — where the Father did not withhold his only Son (Romans 8:32 deliberately echoes Genesis 22:16), and no ram was provided.
1: God tested Abraham — the testing (nasah) is the theological frame that the narrator places explicitly before the event. This is not entrapment but covenant-refining: “when I have tested him, he will come forth as gold” (Job 23:10; cf. 1 Peter 1:7: “your faith — of greater worth than gold, which perishes even though refined by fire”). Christ himself is tested in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1: “Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil”) and in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:36–46). The divine testing of the covenant-bearer is not divine cruelty but the means by which the faithfulness of the tested is revealed and confirmed.
2: “Take your son, your only son, whom you love — Isaac — and go to the region of Moriah. Sacrifice him there as a burnt offering” — the triple intensification of the command (“your son, your only son, whom you love”) is the verbal texture that John 3:16 echoes: “For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son.” The language of God’s command to Abraham is the language John uses for God’s own giving of Christ. The father who loves his son but is commanded to give him is the human type of the Father who loves the Son and gives him for the world’s salvation. Moriah is identified in 2 Chronicles 3:1 as the site of Solomon’s temple in Jerusalem — the mountain where the ultimate sacrifice is eventually offered.
3: Early next morning Abraham got up and loaded his donkey, took two servants and his son Isaac, cut the wood, and set out — Abraham’s prompt obedience without visible hesitation. The early morning rising mirrors the early morning entry of the crucifixion narrative (Mark 15:1: “Very early in the morning, the chief priests… reached a decision”). The prompt, unflinching movement toward the place of sacrifice despite its cost is the type of Christ’s “resolutely setting out for Jerusalem” (Luke 9:51: “As the time approached for him to be taken up to heaven, Jesus resolutely set out for Jerusalem”). Both the type and the antitype move toward the sacrifice with deliberate, early-morning determination.
4: On the third day Abraham looked up and saw the place in the distance — the third-day arrival at the place of sacrifice. The three-day pattern is everywhere in Scripture: Jonah in the great fish three days (Matthew 12:40: “For as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of a huge fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth”); Israel at Sinai to prepare for God’s appearance (Exodus 19:11, 15); Esther’s three-day fast before approaching the king. Hebrews 11:19 says Abraham reasoned that God could raise Isaac from the dead — “in a manner of speaking he did receive Isaac back from the dead” on the third day. The third-day arrival at Moriah is the type of the third-day resurrection.
5: Abraham said to his servants, “Stay here with the donkey while I and the boy go over there. We will worship and then we will come back to you” — “We will come back” (plural): Abraham speaks in faith that both he and Isaac will return. Whether Abraham expects God to stop the sacrifice or to raise Isaac afterward, his faith in God’s power to fulfill the promise through Isaac (cf. Hebrews 11:17–19) is the ground of this plural. The plural “we” is the prophetic faith-statement that the disciples experience on Easter morning: Jesus says “I will go ahead of you into Galilee” (Matthew 26:32) — the confidence that the sacrifice does not end in death but in return. Faith speaks of the return before the sacrifice.
6: Abraham took the wood for the burnt offering and placed it on his son Isaac, and he himself carried the fire and the knife — Isaac carrying the wood for the burnt offering on which he will be offered. The image is one of the most striking types in the OT: the son carries the instrument of his own sacrifice to the place of offering. John 19:17: “Carrying his own cross, he went out to the place of the Skull (which in Aramaic is called Golgotha).” As Isaac carried the wood (= the cross), Jesus carried his cross. The wood that Isaac bears is the means of execution prepared for him; Christ carries the cross, the means of execution that was prepared for him. The correspondence is precise and deliberate in the typological tradition.
7: Isaac spoke up: “Father?” “Yes, my son?” “The fire and wood are here, but where is the lamb for the burnt offering?” — Isaac’s question is the question that the entire sacrificial system asks. The lamb is always the central figure: Passover lamb, daily burnt offering lambs, Day of Atonement sacrifices, the servant of Isaiah 53:7 (“like a lamb led to the slaughter”). John 1:29: “Look, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!” — John the Baptist’s cry is the answer to Isaac’s question. Where is the lamb? Every sacrifice pointed forward to the answer that arrives in Christ. “Where is the lamb?” is the OT’s deepest question; “Behold the Lamb of God” is the NT’s answer.
8: Abraham answered, “God himself will provide the lamb for the burnt offering, my son” — the prophetic faith-statement that is both immediately true (the ram in v13) and ultimately true at a depth Abraham does not yet see. The statement “God himself will provide” is the theological center of the Akedah and the OT’s clearest prefiguration of the doctrine of substitutionary atonement: God does not require the sacrifice of human children; God provides the sacrifice himself. “He who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all” (Romans 8:32) — God provides not a ram for Abraham but his own Son for sinners. The “God himself will provide” is the OT promise that the cross fulfills.
9: Abraham built the altar, arranged the wood, bound his son Isaac, and laid him on the altar on top of the wood — the binding of Isaac (the “Akedah”) is the central image of the chapter. Isaac’s submission to being bound and placed on the altar is the type of Christ’s voluntary submission to the cross. “No one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord” (John 10:18). Both Isaac and Christ submit voluntarily — Isaac could likely have resisted his elderly father, but he does not; Christ “humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross” (Philippians 2:8). The son’s willing submission to the father’s will is the typological heart of the Akedah.
10: Abraham reached out his hand and took the knife to slay his son — the moment of maximum obedience, the nadir before the divine intervention. Hebrews 11:17 says Abraham “offered up Isaac” (perfect tense — the act was complete in his heart and intention before the ram was provided). The willingness to sacrifice the beloved son is the complete test of covenant loyalty; it is also the type of the Father’s willingness in the gospel: “God so loved the world that he gave” (John 3:16). The raised knife is the moment before which the type stops — God stops Abraham — because the substitution is provided. But the knife does not stop at Calvary; the Father does not spare the Son (Romans 8:32).
11: The angel of the LORD called out from heaven, “Abraham! Abraham!” “Here I am” — the double-name call (“Abraham! Abraham!”) marks a moment of highest urgency and divine intensity: “Moses! Moses!” at the burning bush (Exodus 3:4), “Samuel! Samuel!” at the divine call (1 Samuel 3:10), “Simon, Simon!” before Peter’s denial (Luke 22:31), “Saul, Saul!” on the Damascus road (Acts 9:4). The doubled name signals a turning point in the divine-human relationship. The angel calling from heaven corresponds to the Father’s voice from heaven at Christ’s baptism (Matthew 3:17) and transfiguration (Matthew 17:5): the divine voice from heaven authenticating the covenant relationship at the critical moment.
12: “Do not do anything to him. Now I know that you fear God, because you have not withheld from me your son, your only son” — the divine recognition of Abraham’s obedience. “You have not withheld… your son, your only son” — the same triple identification as v2, now turned into a statement of what Abraham has given. Paul uses this language for God’s action in Christ: “He who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all” (Romans 8:32 — “spare” = held back = withheld). What God asks Abraham to give, and what Abraham gives willingly, God gives for real in the gospel: the beloved Son not withheld. The stopping of the sacrifice in v12 is the type’s limit; in Christ, the Father does not stop.
14: Abraham called that place “The LORD Will Provide” (YHWH-Jireh). “On the mountain of the LORD it will be provided” — the naming of Moriah as the place of divine provision is a covenant name that carries forward: “on the mountain of the LORD it will be provided.” The future passive (“it will be provided”) is both a name-etiology and a prophecy: the provision is not exhausted by the ram but points to a future provision on the same mountain. 2 Chronicles 3:1 identifies Moriah as the site of Solomon’s temple in Jerusalem — and Jerusalem is where Christ was crucified. On the mountain of the LORD, the ultimate provision is indeed provided: the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. YHWH-Jireh is fulfilled at Golgotha.
15: The angel of the LORD called to Abraham from heaven a second time — the second calling signals a new, heightened covenant declaration following the test. The double call from heaven corresponds to the double witness pattern of OT law (Deuteronomy 19:15: “a matter must be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses”). The second calling introduces the covenant oath of vv16–18, which is the most solemn renewal of the Abrahamic promise in the narrative. Hebrews 6:17–18 uses this oath to assure believers: “God wanted to make the unchanging nature of his purpose very clear… by two unchangeable things in which it is impossible for God to lie.”
16: “I swear by myself, declares the LORD, that because you have done this and have not withheld your son, your only son” — God swears by himself because there is no greater oath-giver. Hebrews 6:13: “When God made his promise to Abraham, since there was no one greater for him to swear by, he swore by himself.” The divine self-oath establishes the absolute security of the promise. The oath is a response to Abraham’s obedience — not that Abraham earned the promise, but that his faithfulness is the occasion for the solemn covenant renewal. Paul in Romans 4 is careful to show that the oath-based covenant is prior to and independent of the law, so that it comes “by faith… in order that it may be by grace” (Romans 4:16).
17: “I will surely bless you and make your descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky and as the sand on the seashore. Your descendants will take possession of the cities of their enemies” — the dual promise: innumerable offspring and military victory. Paul in Galatians 3:29 identifies the believers in Christ as the fulfillment of the innumerable-offspring promise: “If you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham’s seed, and heirs according to the promise.” The “taking possession of the cities of their enemies” finds its spiritual fulfillment in Christ’s declaration: “the gates of Hades will not overcome” the church he builds (Matthew 16:18). The military victory over enemies is fulfilled in Christ’s conquest of sin, death, and the devil (Colossians 2:15).
18: “Through your offspring all nations on earth will be blessed, because you have obeyed me” — the universal blessing through Abraham’s offspring. Paul quotes this in Galatians 3:8 as the gospel announced in advance: “the Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith, and announced the gospel in advance to Abraham: ‘All nations will be blessed through you.’” The “offspring” through whom all nations are blessed is identified by Paul in Galatians 3:16 as Christ. The mission of the church to all nations (Matthew 28:19) is the outworking of this promise: through Christ, Abraham’s single offspring, the Akedah-born blessing flows to every nation, tribe, people, and language (Revelation 7:9).
19: Abraham returned to his servants, and they set off together for Beersheba. And Abraham stayed in Beersheba — the return after the sacrifice and the covenant renewal. The return — with Isaac — is itself the type-fulfillment that Hebrews 11:19 notes: Abraham “received Isaac back from the dead.” After the Akedah, Isaac is alive where he might have been dead; after the cross, Christ is alive where death expected him to stay dead. Both returns are to the same community (the servants; the disciples), both are unexpected reversals of the logic of sacrifice, both inaugurate a new phase of covenant life. The return to Beersheba after Moriah is the type of the return to Galilee (Matthew 28:10) after Golgotha.
20: After these things Abraham was told that Milcah had borne sons to his brother Nahor — the genealogical report that introduces Rebekah’s family (v23: Bethuel became the father of Rebekah). The narrative pivot from the Akedah to the genealogy is unexpected but purposeful: immediately after the most intense test of the covenant promise, the narrative provides the next step toward its fulfillment. Rebekah will become Isaac’s wife (Genesis 24), continuing the covenant line. The genealogy after the Akedah is the narrative’s way of saying: the covenant continues; the sacrifice does not end the story. After the cross, the genealogy of the church (Acts 2–28) is the continuation that the death could not stop.
21: Uz the firstborn and Buz his brother — Nahor’s sons. Uz is the home of Job (Job 1:1); Buz is the origin of Elihu (Job 32:2). The narrative’s genealogical lists connect figures across the canon: Job’s suffering and questioning connects the patriarchal era to the wisdom literature’s wrestling with God. Job’s anticipation of a mediator-redeemer (Job 9:33: “if only there were someone to mediate between us”; Genesis 19:25: “I know that my redeemer lives”) is one of the OT’s most explicit longings for Christ as the advocate who stands between the suffering human and the holy God. The Uz connection places Job within the Abrahamic family network.
22: Kesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel — the remaining sons of Nahor by Milcah. These names are otherwise unrecorded in Scripture except as ancestors. The genealogy is the canonical-context note that situates the covenant family in its extended network of relationships. All the genealogical lists in Genesis are steps in the narrowing path from Adam to Noah to Shem to Abraham to Isaac to Jacob to Judah to David to Christ (Matthew 1:1–17; Luke 3:23–38). The names that do not appear in the direct covenant line are still part of the providential landscape through which Christ’s lineage is preserved and prepared.
23: Bethuel became the father of Rebekah. Milcah bore these eight sons to Abraham’s brother Nahor — the key genealogical information: Rebekah is introduced. Rebekah will be Isaac’s wife (Genesis 24:67), the mother of Jacob and Esau, and the next woman through whom the covenant promise is passed. Paul in Romans 9:10–12 uses Rebekah’s pregnancy as the key example of election by grace: “Yet, before the twins were born or had done anything good or bad — in order that God’s purpose in election might stand: not by works but by him who calls — she was told, ‘The older will serve the younger.’” Rebekah’s introduction here is the genealogical step that leads to Jacob, then to the twelve tribes, then to Judah, then to David, then to Christ.
24: His concubine Reumah also had sons: Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, and Maakah — Nahor’s sons by his concubine. The secondary line through the concubine — parallel to the Hagar/Ishmael situation — is a canonical-context note showing that the covenant promise runs through specific primary lines, not through all descendants equally. The narrowing of the covenant from all Noah’s descendants to Shem, from Shem to Abraham, from Abraham to Isaac, from Isaac to Jacob, and ultimately to the one seed who is Christ (Galatians 3:16) proceeds by divine election and promise, not by natural birth order or status. The concubine’s sons are part of the world through which the covenant moves, not the channel through which it flows.
